summary: mailconfirmer postinstall drops and executes XOR-encrypted Windows binary, then erases itself from manifests
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall hook (install-hook.js) runs a base64-encoded UTF-16LE PowerShell payload via `powershell -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Enc <base64>`. The decoded script downloads `launcher.bin` from `https://github.com/Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note/releases/download/v2/launcher.bin` — a personal GitHub repository unrelated to the package's advertised email-confirmation purpose — XOR-decrypts the bytes with key 0x42, writes the result to `%TEMP%\tmp.exe`, launches it hidden via `Start-Process -WindowStyle Hidden`, sleeps, and deletes the dropped file. The fetched payload is opaque (XOR-obfuscated), unpinned by hash, and served from a maintainer account that does not match the npm publisher. After execution, the hook performs anti-forensics: it edits the consumer's `package.json` and `package-lock.json` to remove the `mailconfirmer` entry (`$pkg.dependencies.PSObject.Properties.Remove('mailconfirmer')`), and schedules the installed `node_modules/mailconfirmer` directory for deletion on next reboot using `MoveFileEx` with `MOVEFILE_DELAY_UNTIL_REBOOT` (0x4), so a developer auditing dependencies cannot trace where the payload came from. This is a deliberate supply-chain dropper attack triggered automatically on default install on Windows hosts.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
